title: Quantifier expressions (forall/exists)
analysis: This task adds forall/exists quantifier expressions across the full pipeline: grammar -> parser -> compiler -> evaluator. The pattern closely follows the lambda expression implementation, which already binds a variable in scope and evaluates a body. The quantifier is simpler than lambda because it doesn't produce a closure value — it iterates a collection and evaluates a predicate with the bound variable. The key changes are: (1) grammar.js: add `quantifier_expression` rule with `forall`/`exists` keyword, identifier, `in`, collection expression, `:`, predicate expression; (2) reify-syntax: add `ExprKind::Quantifier` with `QuantifierKind` enum and lowering in ts_parser.rs; (3) reify-types/expr.rs: add `CompiledExprKind::Quantifier` variant with collection and predicate compiled exprs, plus update `walk()`, `collect_value_refs()`, and add a constructor; (4) reify-compiler: compile the quantifier by creating a new scope for the bound variable (like lambda), compiling collection and predicate, and capturing the variable binding; (5) reify-expr: evaluate by iterating the collection, binding each element, evaluating the predicate, with Kleene 3-valued logic and short-circuit behavior. The existing method `all`/`any` on lists already implements the Kleene logic pattern, and `apply_lambda` shows how to bind variables in scope for evaluation — the quantifier eval will follow this same pattern but more directly.

prerequisites.description: Add quantifier_expression rule to tree-sitter grammar.js, regenerate parser.c with `tree-sitter generate`

steps.description: Write failing parse tests in crates/reify-syntax/tests/quantifier_tests.rs: parse `forall x in list: x > 0` -> Quantifier(ForAll), parse `exists x in set: x == target` -> Quantifier(Exists)

Add QuantifierKind enum (ForAll/Exists) and ExprKind::Quantifier variant to reify-syntax/src/lib.rs. Add 'quantifier_expression' lowering to ts_parser.rs lower_expr() match. Add the new node kind to the lower_expr dispatch.

Write failing compile tests in crates/reify-compiler/tests/quantifier_compile_tests.rs: compile `forall x in [1,2,3]: x > 0` -> CompiledExprKind::Quantifier with ForAll kind, verify collection and predicate sub-expressions are compiled

Add CompiledExprKind::Quantifier variant to reify-types/src/expr.rs with QuantifierKind, variable: String, variable_id: ValueCellId, collection: Box<CompiledExpr>, predicate: Box<CompiledExpr>. Add QuantifierKind enum (ForAll/Exists) to reify-types. Add constructor, update walk(), collect_value_refs_inner(). Add ExprKind::Quantifier compilation in reify-compiler: create nested scope with bound variable, compile collection and predicate.

Write failing eval tests in crates/reify-expr/tests/quantifier_eval_tests.rs: forall over [1,2,3] with x>0 -> true; forall over [1,-1,3] with x>0 -> false; exists over [1,2,3] with x>2 -> true; exists over [1,2,3] with x>5 -> false

Add CompiledExprKind::Quantifier evaluation in reify-expr/src/lib.rs eval_expr(): evaluate collection, iterate elements, bind variable in scope, evaluate predicate. ForAll: conjunction (all true -> true, any false -> false). Exists: disjunction (any true -> true, all false -> false).
Write failing eval tests for edge cases: forall over empty list -> true (vacuous truth); exists over empty list -> false (vacuous falsity)
Ensure empty collection handling is correct in quantifier eval (likely already works from step-6 implementation if using proper fold semantics)

Write failing eval tests for Kleene 3-valued logic: forall over [1, Undef, 3] with x>0 -> Undef (no false, but undef present); exists over [1, Undef, 3] with x>2 -> true (short-circuit on 3>2=true); exists over [Undef, -1] with x>0 -> Undef (no true, undef present)

Ensure Kleene 3-valued logic in quantifier eval: forall short-circuits on false, tracks undef; exists short-circuits on true, tracks undef. Mirror the pattern from eval_and/eval_or and the existing .all()/.any() method implementations.
Write failing integration test: quantifier in constraint context - parse+compile+eval `constraint forall b in bolts: b.grade >= 8.8` inside a structure with a list-typed let binding

Ensure quantifier works end-to-end in constraint context. Fix any issues with the collection type being resolved correctly, member access on bound variable within predicate, and update collect_body_refs in the compiler for the new Quantifier variant.

Write failing test `lambda_containing_forall_has_correct_captures` in crates/reify-compiler/tests/quantifier_compile_tests.rs: compile a lambda that wraps a quantifier, e.g. `|items| forall x in items: x > 0`, and assert the lambda's `captures` vec contains only `items`'s ValueCellId and does NOT contain the quantifier's bound `x` variable ID (the `$quant0.S:x` synthetic cell). This exposes the bug in `collect_body_refs_inner` where the Quantifier arm blindly recurses into the predicate without filtering out `variable_id`, causing the quantifier's bound variable to leak into the lambda's capture list.

Fix `collect_body_refs_inner` in crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs for the `CompiledExprKind::Quantifier` arm (lines 2561-2564). Instead of blindly recursing into both `collection` and `predicate`, mirror the filtering logic already present in `collect_value_refs_inner` (reify-types/src/expr.rs lines 362-372): recurse into `collection` normally, but collect `predicate` refs into a temporary vec and filter out the bound `variable_id` before appending to `refs`. This prevents the quantifier's bound variable from leaking into lambda capture lists.

design_decisions.decision: QuantifierKind enum lives in reify-types alongside other compiled expression types
design_decisions.rationale: reify-types is the shared types crate used by both reify-compiler and reify-expr. The QuantifierKind enum is needed in CompiledExprKind (reify-types/expr.rs) and in ExprKind (reify-syntax). We need it in both places. For the AST side it lives in reify-syntax, for the compiled side in reify-types.

design_decisions.decision: Quantifier bound variable uses ValueCellId with a synthetic entity name (like lambda params)
design_decisions.rationale: The lambda implementation uses `$lambda{N}.{entity}` as the entity name for lambda parameters. Quantifiers will use `$quant{N}.{entity}` for the bound variable. This ensures unique ValueCellIds that don't clash with structure members or other variables.

design_decisions.decision: Quantifier evaluation directly binds the variable in a ValueMap scope rather than creating a Lambda closure
design_decisions.rationale: While quantifiers could theoretically desugar to collection.all(|x| predicate) or collection.any(|x| predicate), a direct implementation is cleaner, avoids the overhead of closure creation per element, and gives better error messages. The .all()/.any() methods already exist for lambda-based usage.

design_decisions.decision: Quantifier works on both List and Set types
design_decisions.rationale: The task spec says collection must be List/Set type. For Set, we iterate the BTreeSet. This aligns with the language semantics where quantifiers make sense over any iterable collection.

design_decisions.decision: Grammar uses colon separator between collection and predicate: `forall x in coll: pred`
design_decisions.rationale: The task spec uses this syntax. The colon is unambiguous here since it follows an expression (the collection) and precedes another expression (the predicate). No conflict with existing colon uses (type annotations always follow identifiers/params, not expressions).

design_decisions.decision: Content hash tag byte 13 for Quantifier CompiledExpr constructor
design_decisions.rationale: Existing constructors use tag bytes 0-12. The next available is 13 for the Quantifier variant, maintaining the convention of unique tag bytes per expression kind.

design_decisions.decision: Grammar conflict: 'in' keyword clashes with port_direction_keyword
design_decisions.rationale: The port_direction_keyword already uses 'in' as a keyword. The quantifier uses 'in' in a different syntactic position (after identifier in quantifier context). Tree-sitter can disambiguate these positionally since quantifier_expression starts with 'forall'/'exists' keyword. May need to add quantifier_expression to the conflicts list or use token precedence if conflicts arise.

design_decisions.decision: collect_body_refs_inner must filter quantifier bound variable from predicate refs (same as collect_value_refs_inner)
design_decisions.rationale: The compiler has two ref-collection functions: collect_value_refs_inner (in reify-types) and collect_body_refs_inner (in reify-compiler). The former correctly filters out the quantifier's variable_id from predicate refs, but the latter was missing this filter. When a lambda body contains a quantifier, collect_body_refs is used for capture analysis, and the unfiltered bound variable leaks into the captures vec. Both functions must apply the same filtering logic for correctness.

reuse.what: Lambda compilation pattern (nested scope with bound variable)
reuse.how: Follow the same pattern: create a new entity name for the quantifier scope, register the bound variable with a ValueCellId, compile the predicate in the extended scope. Reuse CompilationScope.clone() + names.insert() for variable binding.
The existing .all() and .any() method implementations on lists already implement the exact Kleene logic needed for forall/exists. Mirror the same short-circuit + undef tracking pattern.
Follow the same test structure: construct CompiledExpr trees directly, eval with EvalContext::simple, assert Value results.
The quantifier_expression follows a similar pattern of keyword + params + body. Add it to _expression choices alongside lambda_expression.
Add Quantifier variant to both methods: walk collection and predicate, collect refs from collection and predicate (excluding the bound variable_id from predicate refs, similar to lambda captures).
Reuse the same helper pattern for quantifier compile tests: parse source, compile, assert no errors, inspect compiled expression kind.
Reuse the same helper to parse source with a structure wrapper and extract members for assertion.

The fix for collect_body_refs_inner directly mirrors this existing pattern: collect predicate refs into a temp vec, filter out variable_id, then extend the main refs vec. The code structure is identical.
